name: Smith v. Jones Mercantile Co.
headnotes: Where a buyer repudiates before the delivery date, the seller may treat the contract as breached and recover expectation damages measured by the contract price less resale proceeds.
snippet: A refusal to accept goods on the agreed date, without legal excuse, constitutes anticipatory breach. The non-breaching party need not await the performance date to sue.

name: Williams v. Central Railroad
headnotes: Contributory negligence bars recovery where the plaintiff's conduct is a proximate cause of the injury and fails to exercise ordinary care.
snippet: The railroad owed a duty of reasonable care at crossings, but the plaintiff's failure to stop, look, and listen when approaching a known dangerous crossing defeats the claim.

name: Thompson v. Greenacre Estates
headnotes: Specific performance is appropriate for land contracts because each parcel is unique; money damages are presumptively inadequate.
snippet: Where the contract clearly describes the tract and the buyer is ready, willing, and able to close, equity will compel conveyance absent a valid defense.

name: Davis v. Ozark Manufacturing
headnotes: Termination without cause during a definite employment term breaches the contract; damages are measured by wages for the unexpired term minus mitigation earnings.
snippet: An at-will presumption does not apply where the parties fixed a one-year term in writing. Summary discharge without cause supports recovery of unpaid wages for the remainder.

name: Miller v. Pioneer Investments
headnotes: Fraud in the inducement requires a material misrepresentation, scienter, reasonable reliance, and resulting damages.
snippet: General puffery about investment returns, without specific false statements of fact, does not establish fraudulent inducement where the buyer had access to financial records.

name: Brown v. City Contractors
headnotes: Where a contract is unenforceable but benefits were conferred in good faith, quantum meruit may recover the reasonable value of services.
snippet: The subcontractor completed work accepted by the owner; failure to satisfy the statute of frauds does not permit retention of the benefit without payment.
